# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for a React application. Install Node.js, run npm install, build the production bundle with npm run build, and serve it with a lightweight HTTP server. Keep the Dockerfile simple and easy to understand for our team.
# Category: container_security

FROM node:14-alpine AS builder  # use alpine image since we're only using Node.js (not npm or other tools) that aren't updated regularly, this can save a lot of space during build times when deploying to Heroku/AWS for example
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./  # copy over the lock file from our base image and add these as dependencies in each subsequent COPY operations. This avoids installing npm packages on every single run (speed up builds) thanks to a shared build environment across multiple layers within one Dockerfile step, this will be executed once when we 'docker build'
RUN apk update && apk upgrade  # make sure the node_modules are updated before install happens. This avoids conflicts with external sources that might not properly manage their dependencies (i.e., using non-standard Node packages) at runtime due to alpine images only providing a minimal subset of what's available in standard image
RUN npm install  # this will handle the rest - installing all our application’s dependencies, i.e.: 'react', then saving any node_modules into /app during build phase (to be included next time), and starting up an http server to serve out static files from within docker container at localhost:3001
COPY . ./  # the rest of these copies will copy everything we have on top level in our current directory - all source code, test data & assets. This is done as much once when 'docker build'ing (it only happens first time), and then repeatedly during every subsequent run with a fresh Docker container setup for each application rebuild
RUN npm run build  # this runs the production bundle that we’ve built - to generate static files, ie., HTML/CSS etc. which is what will be served by our server below (to serve at http://localhost:3001) when 'docker start'ing a new container from setup
EXPOSE 3001   # this means that the app can receive requests on port :80, and thus only then should we see logs. To view log data within docker console via CTRL-C if it’s stopped or terminated by some signal like SIGKILL/SIGTERM
ENTRYPOINT ["node", "server.js"]  # this means that when a user uses 'docker run' command, the ENTRYPOINT (i.e., our server script) should start executing at port :3001 and serve everything within container context to users who are requesting these services
